summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the /rest/workflows/from-url endpoint makes an unvalidated server-side HTTP GET request to an attacker-controlled URL. A mock internal service running inside the container receives the request from the n8n server, proving the SSRF without any SSRF protection applied.
expected_security_impact: The n8n server should make an HTTP GET request to the mock internal service at http://127.0.0.1:9999/ssrf-proof, and the internal service should record the incoming request with its headers, proving that the server can reach internal network resources.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the Docker image, started n8n, initialized an owner account, created a project, and then called GET /rest/workflows/from-url?url=http://127.0.0.1:9999/ssrf-proof. The mock internal service received the request from the n8n server process and wrote a proof marker file. The endpoint returned the mock response data (a fake workflow JSON) confirming data exfiltration from the internal service.
verification_method: The verify.sh script starts a mock HTTP server that listens on port 9999 and writes a proof marker file (/tmp/ssrf-proof-marker) containing the request details when it receives any request to /ssrf-proof. After triggering the SSRF, the script checks for the existence and content of the proof marker file. This distinguishes a real SSRF from a failed startup or network error.
evidence_produced_by_run: The mock internal service received an HTTP GET request to /ssrf-proof from the n8n server process.
The proof marker file /tmp/ssrf-proof-marker was created with the request timestamp and source IP.
The /rest/workflows/from-url endpoint returned the response body from the internal service (a crafted workflow JSON), confirming data exfiltration.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the n8n repository on the host, checks out the pinned version tag, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
